summary: Nubuwwa is the Islamic doctrine of prophethood: the belief that God appoints chosen human beings to receive and communicate His guidance to humanity. It is a core article of Islamic belief and, in systematic theology (kalam), the subject of a distinct treatise.
description: Nubuwwa holds that God, out of grace, sends prophets (sing. nabi) to guide humanity, and that some prophets are also messengers (sing. rasul) entrusted with a scripture or law. Classical theologians commonly distinguished the nabi, who receives revelation, from the rasul, who is additionally sent with a message or law to a community, every rasul being a nabi, but not conversely. Kalam treated prophethood as a rationally examinable institution, addressing questions such as why prophethood is necessary, how a genuine prophet is recognized, and what protects a prophet from error. A prophet's claim was classically held to be authenticated by an evidentiary miracle (mu'jiza), an act exceeding human capacity that accompanies and confirms the claim to be sent by God. Many theologians also affirmed the doctrine of 'isma, the divinely granted protection of prophets from sin or error, though the scope of that protection was debated across schools. The theological schools differed on the underlying rationale: Mu'tazili theologians, emphasizing divine justice and human reason, tended to argue that sending prophets serves an ascertainable good, while Ash'ari theologians grounded prophethood in God's will and framed the miracle as the decisive sign. In Islamic doctrine Muhammad is affirmed as the last of the prophets (khatam al-nabiyyin), the seal of prophethood.
editorial_notes: This entry is scoped as an introduction to nubuwwa as a topic of kalam. The nabi/rasul distinction, the miracle (mu'jiza) as proof, 'isma (prophetic protection from sin), and the finality of prophethood are named as classical themes without adjudicating the school-specific disputes over each (e.g. the precise scope of 'isma, or whether prophethood is rationally obligatory upon God). Coverage of Shi'i prophetology (e.g. its close linkage to imama/wilaya) is deferred to dedicated entries. To triangulate the Western academic references with the tradition's own kalām literature (neutrality principle #8), al-Juwaynī's al-Irshād (in Walker's translation) is appended at medium confidence for the Ashʿarī treatment of prophethood and the muʿjiza; its relevant passages were not read line-by-line here. Source-quality pass (neutrality principle #9, 2026-07-18): the Encyclopaedia of Islam (Wensinck et al.) entry was removed as a reference work. Re-sourcing pass (2026-07-18, peer-review follow-up): to correct a Sunni-skewed two-source base, Sarah Stroumsa's peer-reviewed article 'The Signs of Prophecy' (Harvard Theological Review, 1985) was added as a study of kalām prophetology and the muʿjiza; it also carries the Muʿtazili rationale for prophethood (via ʿAbd al-Jabbār's argument that only the miracle proves the sending of messengers), at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line. The entry now rests on one academic monograph (the Cambridge Companion), one peer-reviewed article (Stroumsa), and one classical primary text in critical translation. Third-lens pass (2026-07-18): a modern/contemporary Muslim scholarly source has been added, Fazlur Rahman's monograph Prophecy in Islam: Philosophy and Orthodoxy (1958), devoted to the doctrine of nubuwwa and its philosophical and orthodox treatments. The entry now triangulates three lenses: Western academic (Winter ed., Stroumsa), the tradition's own classical kalām text (al-Juwaynī), and a modern Muslim scholar (Rahman), which converge on prophethood as a rationally examinable institution centered on revelation and the authentication of the prophet's claim, without adjudicating the school-specific disputes. VERIFIED (2026-07-19): the load-bearing Qur'anic verses (Q 16:36, 4:163-165, 21:25, 2:285, 33:40) were read directly at quran.com and confirmed to carry the entry's core (messengers sent to every community, revelation to a lineage of prophets, Muhammad as the seal); a Qur'an source is added at high, and the entry is promoted to verified. The kalam claims (miracle as proof, isma, the Mu'tazili and Ash'ari rationale) remain attributed at medium (their sources not read line by line).

relations.note: Prophethood delivers and safeguards the message of God's oneness.
Nubuwwa is classically enumerated among the foundational articles of belief.
The evidentiary miracle classically authenticates a prophet's claim.
Prophetic protection from sin or error.
The finality/seal of prophethood affirmed of Muhammad.

historical_period.note: Rooted in the earliest Qur'anic revelation; developed as a distinct treatise (nubuwwat) by the theological schools from roughly the 8th–10th centuries CE onward.

sources.citation: The Qur'an, passages on prophethood and messengership: Q 16:36 ('to every community We sent a messenger, "Worship God, and avoid idolatry"'), Q 4:163-165 (revelation to a lineage of prophets; messengers as bearers of good news and warning), Q 21:25 (every messenger inspired that there is no god but God), Q 2:285 (belief in God, His angels, His books, His messengers), and Q 33:40 (Muhammad 'the seal of the prophets', khatam al-nabiyyin). The primary scriptural basis for the sending of prophets and the seal.
sources.license: Primary religious text; classical Arabic in the public domain. Verses read directly (2026-07-19) and confirmed.

sources.citation: Winter, T. (ed.), The Cambridge Companion to Classical Islamic Theology. Cambridge University Press, 2008.

al-Juwaynī, Imām al-Ḥaramayn. Kitāb al-Irshād ilā Qawāṭiʿ al-Adilla fī Uṣūl al-Iʿtiqād [A Guide to Conclusive Proofs for the Principles of Belief], trans. Paul E. Walker. Reading: Garnet Publishing, 2000 (Great Books of Islamic Civilisation). Ashʿarī kalām treatise (11th c. CE) treating prophethood (nubuwwa), the possibility and demonstration of its existence, and the evidentiary miracle (muʿjiza) as the sign authenticating a prophet's claim.

Stroumsa, Sarah. 'The Signs of Prophecy: The Emergence and Early Development of a Theme in Arabic Theological Literature.' Harvard Theological Review 78, no. 1-2 (1985): 101-114. Peer-reviewed study of how the evidentiary miracle (muʿjiza / dalāʾil al-nubuwwa) developed as the criterion for authenticating a prophet's claim in early Arabic kalām, including the Muʿtazili rationale (e.g. ʿAbd al-Jabbār's position that only miracles constitute a proper proof for the sending of messengers).
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Rahman, Fazlur. Prophecy in Islam: Philosophy and Orthodoxy. London: George Allen and Unwin, 1958; repr. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1979. A monograph on the doctrine of prophethood (nubuwwa) by a leading modern Muslim scholar, tracing the philosophers' theory of prophecy and its varying reception by kalām orthodoxy, including revelation, the prophet's cognition, and the confirmation of a prophet's claim. Added as the modern Muslim scholarly lens on nubuwwa; cited at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.
